In the video "15 Insane CSE Projects...", it was suggested to create a web development project that combines JavaScript with neural networks. The idea is to build a game in JavaScript and have a bot learn to play it, with the goal of beating the high score. The recommended approach was to use the JavaScript canvas for the game.

I am considering a similar concept but on a larger scale. My plan is to develop a 2D open-world pixel game where NPCs and vehicles are controlled by AI bots. Each bot would have a specific goal and attempt to achieve it, improving its performance over time. For this purpose, I am leaning towards using Phaser.js. From my observation, Phaser.js is more suitable for scaling up complex projects, whereas working directly with the canvas tends to make the code longer and more difficult to manage.

But what's your take on this?

hello fellas, I have a question to the fellow tech nerds
You know how in machine learning we use CUDA (in other words Nvidia GPUs) to train models given their high capacity of computing compared to CPUs
Has anyone tried to configure a GPU alternative instead of working with highly performing CPUs? I'm talking architecture level

i mean are you asking can we use a high performing cpu as an alternative to GPU

if that's the question you can't


cpu is  designed for doing heavy task, if you multiple cores with threads you can get maximum number of maths calc it can handle per second

gpu is designed for light task parlelly, it can run lot of small ops per second.

technically gpu and cpu are same processing chips. but built for differnt things

gpu can we used as cpu if each core is power enough, and cpu can do gpu task if it's power enough.


and there are integrated graphics in cpu processors too.
they cut one quater into small small parts and use it for doing gpu stuff.

ah that's interesting, what I was talking about is basically the concept of GPU acceleration. The idea is to take the load off CPUs and use GPU instead. I'm aware that the design of Gpus is mostly graphical and for parallel workflows, but I think with some tinkering it is possible to use the pc's biggest processing unit to its full potential. 
Similarly to how RAPIDS did it for Data science/analytics tasks

yeah if you're doing lot of small task you can optimize to put that load into gpu instead of cpu.

example:
choosing render using gpu or cpu in blender
davinci resolve vs permiere pro perfomance.


it's more about the underlying software and how they code.
we can surely offload task from cpu to gpu.

I completely agree, however I'm leaning more into programming.

The spark behind this idea is that when I see Data Engineers working on deploying their architecture they calculate how much CPUs they'd need as well as RAM. While working with big data they begin allocating huge number of CPUs to handle the workloads but I think using GPUs would be far more efficient given the simplicity of their tasks. (SQL operations for example) and the compatibility of most big data libraries (Spark) with GPU acceleration.
Maybe the downside would be the VRAM since the GPU is designed to have low memory but there should be a workaround that
if a operation can be parralelized and each one is not dependent on other and it is quite small. gpu wins

So, I've been building this little command-line tool in Python to search for and keep a personal list of movies and TV shows, all from the terminal.

Here's a breakdown of the tech I'm using and why:
Core App: It's a CLI built with Typer with commands like search, add, and list. Using Rich library for tables and things, for easy to read.
Data Source: It pulls all its data from The Movie Database (TMDB). I wrote a client using HTTPX to talk to their API. The data that comes back from TMDB is parsed and validated using Pydantic, which helps keep things from breaking if the API sends something unexpected.
Database: When you add a movie to your collection, it gets saved into a local database using SQLAlchemy, its overkill rn, but it makes managing the movie data in a structured way easier and leaves room for more complex features later.
Caching: To make searches faster and to avoid spamming the TMDB API every time, I'm using Redis as a cache (this is what motivated me to start a project wanted to try reddis). When you search for something, the results are saved in Redis for a bit. The search command in main.py is set up to check the cache first before making a network request.

Right now, you can search for movies/shows, see details and posters (right in the terminal, but only in kitty you can preview the img in full quality), and add them to your local collection. What do you think? Any suggestions for where to take it next?

Hey everyone,
I’m building an open-source agent-based system for end-to-end data science and would love feedback from this community.

Instead of AutoML pipelines, the system uses multiple agents that mirror how senior data scientists work:

EDA (distributions, imbalance, correlations)

Data cleaning & encoding

Feature engineering (domain features, interactions)

Modeling & validation

Insights & recommendations

The goal is reasoning + explanation, not just metrics.

It’s early-stage and imperfect — I’m specifically looking for:

🐞 bugs and edge cases

⚙️ design or performance improvements

💡 ideas from real-world data workflows

Demo: https://pulastya0-data-science-agent.hf.space/
Repo: https://github.com/Pulastya-B/DevSprint-Data-Science-Agent

Happy to answer questions or discuss architecture choices.

We built RuralCare , a platform aimed at improving healthcare access in rural areas.
What it does: • AI-powered symptom checking
• Doctor consultations via video call (WebRTC)
• AI analysis of medical reports
• Find nearby pharmacies with medicine availability
Built during the hackathon using WebRTC + Groq LLM/Vision.
Would love to get feedback from the community!

#💡-ideas-and-feedback-chat I need review of the idea for a virtual hackathon ...

My idea is basically about creating an agent consent platform. Like, for example, right now there are all the agents have atomic behavior. 
So, like if the agents have all the access or no access, like if you tell an agent to scrap anything, it will scrap any website. But if a website has authentication authorization, it would be difficult to scrap. The other con of this procedure is, if you give your credentials to the AI, then it would be dangerous for you as well. That your credentials have gone to AI completely now it can use or misuse of it.

So, what my solution brings is, I will be using the auth zeros token vault. Now, if I tell an AI agent to scrap the salary slips from a website, what it will do is, it will go and first of all scrap the website. In the website, it will come that login credentials are required, so it will notify the user. The user will add the credentials, and those credentials will be added into the token vault of auth zero in the encrypted format. The AI agent has to use the credentials from the token vault. Now, after that credentials are available, the agent will use the Shorthand, which is developed on the basis of playwright. It will log into the website, crawl it, and it will fetch the details of it, and it will give me the entire data.

Now, the credentials that our human has provided has a validity or a lifetime of 5 minutes, or 10 minutes, like 5 minutes, which is a huge number for an AI. By doing this, a human will always be there in to another group, and since the AI is totally dependent on the open world, once that opens get exhausted, then there is no use of AI of fetching the details from the open world. So, this is how I have planned up creating something for a virtual hackathon.
